note: Hunger... Shyness... Pounding-heart... Keeping-up-appearances are all intense feelings that are produced by or in the kinds of persons mentioned. They introduce a ballad that is about intense feelings, the latter's place in the cosmos, and the kind of action they entail. A child's hunger pains not only the child, but also its caring parents, whose emotion is said to be the referent of this image. A bride's shyness or "shame" is her conventional response to the dramatic transformation in her life produced by change of status and residence at marriage; a bride is an omu-gole and "to be ashamed" is oku-gola (but see also Kitekele:21). The anxiety of warriors and the semi-rich are produced by the social necessity and the possible consequences of public performance. Batende are said to be legendary warriors; the word could also mean "lovers."

Stanza in concatenated types; each line is composed of two nouns, a feeling and a social category characterized by it; syntactically the lines group as aaab: in all but the last line, the relationship between nouns is expressed by the possessive particle /-a/ "of"; in the final line (1.3) the nouns are linked by the conjunction /nka/ "like" or "as"; semantically, the lines associate as aabb according to the social categories - those inside the home, kin (children, brides) versus those outside the home, non-kin (warriors, the semi-rich).

Verse passage composed of three types of linegroups, repeated and elaborated as a unit three times; the component linegroups are: repeated descriptions of lovingly entwined bodies (ENTWINING), passages of praise (PRAISE), and sections of narration or quoted utterance that move the action forward (ACTION; in the first appearance of the triad (21-32) the three parts follow one another linearly: first comes ENTWINING (21-24), internally composed as a couplet (21-21.21-54.1) finalized by a marked word order of direct object + verb, together with a three line stanza (22-24) in aba formed by lines (a's: 22, 24) that have a body part initially and the verb "to be" in mid position contrasted with an intervening line (23) whose (action) verb is in final position, directly preceded by a body part noun; second is PRAISE, in relatively simple couplet form (25-26); finally, ACTION is described from the point of view of an elder woman (28-32) in lines that can be construed as a compound stanza in aab, 2 + 2 + 1: the first couplet (28-29) coheres semantically as discovery/response; the second (30-31) coheres by repetition and parallelism; and the finalizing quoted utterance (32) contrasts with the previous narrative; the second appearance of the triad (ENTWINING, PRAISE, ACTION - 33-42) sees a shortened ENTWINING (33-35) forming a stanza in aba: lines that include verbs (a's: 33, 33.1, 35) are contrasted with one that does not (34); PRAISE and ACTION are themselves entwined, the latter split and encompassing the former in an aba pattern: if they were contiguous, the ACTION lines would form stanza in abab: a's name a social category, girl (36) and boy (41), while b's (37, 42) are both formulated as verb initial + body part; ACTION encapsulates PRAISE (38-40), which forms a stanza in aba formed by contrast between lines that include verbs (38, 40) and one that does not (39); the third appearance of the triad begins with a still shorter ENTWINING (43-44) formed as a stanza in aba' by the contrasting placement of "I found" at the beginning of 43.1; the ACTION is comprised of a judgment regarding what should be done (45-47) structured as aba, verb initial/verb final/verb initial; and the PRAISE is elaborated (48-54.1) into a verse in aba: a's are composed of a repeated formula, question (48, 53), name (49, 54) and praisename (49.1, 54.1); between these stanzas that mirror each other are two linegroups (49.2-51 and 51.1-52.1) the second of which is clearly a stanza in aba formed by familiar lines to which verbs (51.1, 52.1) have markedly been added contrasted with a single intervening line that remains without a verb (52); the three triads that comprise the passage as a whole form an aba pattern; internal cyclical organization of the second triad (33-42) - that is, its encompassing of PRAISE by ACTION (36-42) - is markedly different from the linear organization of the first and last sections, thus creating the aba pattern.

These lines are often sung together by Muzee (see Kajango: 5.2-5.5, 41-41.4, 167.4-167.7), but it seems to me that they form three separate poetic images (those on 1.4 1.5, and 1.6-1.8). All describe intense feelings. A squash plant's stem, tendrils and foliage cover the ground as sinuously as passion envelops the body (see 336). A host's lack of a place to seat guests creates an anxiousness compounded of elevated social standing and insufficient resources (as in 1.3). And the packet of coffee berries has come to embody longing for an absent lover.

I could not till the earth... is the sort of lover's discourse that measures passion against social convention and, by this comparison, indicates the extra-ordinary state that love creates (see The Place You Come From:75-79, 87-98).
These figurative terms praise and address a woman (and below 62, 188).
Soothing-warmth... Bubbling-warmth refer to the warm feeling produced by the pelts of these animals.
Skin... refers to the outer layer of the trunk of a banana plant, which varies in color by species.

These lines were actually sung as:
Kyai k'enkonjwa/ Munyaanya abailagula/ Mh-mh/ Ky'entundu/ Munyaanya abailagula/ Ky'enkonjwa/ Munyanya abela mubili.
Skin of the enkonjwa/ Sister to the dark-complected/ Mh-mh (no)/ Of the entundu/ Sister to the dark-complected/ Of the enkonjwa/ Sister to the light-skinned body.
They are apparantly a mistake and a hasty correction, so I have altered them here to match their repetition on lines 206-209.

Women's pubic hair may be cosmetically removed by shaving as noted here, or by plucking as alluded to at the very end of the ballad (400-404). Most pleasing is said to be the condition a few days after shaving, when the short hair is likened to small, even teeth in a welcoming "smile" - omusheko (15). The image of pubic hair occurs at three critical points in the ballad: beginning, end, and the point at which the stranger wins the bride's heart (247.1).PAGE

Compound stanza, 2 + 2 + 2, in abb; the first line pair (a: 12-13) describes an initial action/reaction pair: knowing, you didn't tell me/ I'd have known; 2nd person/1st person, respectively, in verbal inflections; in both line pairs that follow (b's: 14-15 and 16-16.1) the action/reaction relationships are the reverse; in these, 1st person/2nd person inflections structure the imagined exchange; the second and third line pairs also cohere in that their first lines begin with the formula, verb + body part, and their second lines conclude with the stem /shek/ "laugh" (inflected as noun and verb, respectively).

Grasshopper... and Waves... are metaphors for visual and sensual aspects of female genitals (explained in note to Kachwenyanja: 308) 30, 31: Ilimabuye and Nyakalembe are agricultural spirits to whom women make entreaty (see note to Kajango: 199).

Pathway of Soft-steps is an area of the back near the kidneys. It is the target of tickling jokes between adults of the opposite sex and is said to convey its pleasurable sensations quickly and inwardly (40).

I cannot understand...let them be...born in love. With this interpretation of events the elder avoids the implication that the children's behavior violates natural law. Had they done so, they would have committed an eihano (pl. amahano), a dreaded disturbance in equlibrium that requires redressive action (like the murder in "She's Killed One..." or the unnatural birth in "I Shall Be Drinking From Them" or the incest in "Child of the Valley" in See so...). The unnamed concept surfaces, however, to name the extramarital love that seems a consequence of this early attraction (173, 212, 394). Its semantic element of magical wonder is separated from the element of unnatural calamity on 340.1, where the word evokes sexual attraction. Conversely, the word has only negative, but not unnatural, connotations in Kachwenyanja: 136, 171.

Kaiyula is a woman's name (cf. Ngimbwa's wife in Kaitaba: 110) but the poetic associations of its praise lead in several directions. This is generally true of praise: distinct virtues of similarly named entities intermingle in poetic allusions. As a plant (49.2) ka-iyula can be used to treat headache (Kitekele: 13, 99; there it is called ny-iyula, its prefix changed from the diminutive ka-). But Kaiyula is also a spirit of thunder (Mugasha: 43) and is associated with kito, the second phase of the small dry season (akanda), which precedes the heavy March rains. Thus, she is praised as Little one (Kito) and invited to drop down from above (63, 188.9-188.10).

Compound stanza, 2 + 2 + 1, in aab: two couplets (a's: 57-59 and 60-61) are composed as woman's action/ man's action (line 58, which notes the time of day, seems interpolated); in the single line that finalizes the figure (b: 62) they meet.
Series of 5 couplets formed by parallelism.

Iron points refers to the slim, conical ferrule at the tip of a spearshaft. It is used to plant the spear in the ground with its head up, as a matchmaker does outside the door of a prospective bride's family.

I do not understand the significance of this image. Leather kilts are no longer worn. The dictionary says they were solely the garb of women, (IN:134; see Kitekele: 511) but the destitute clansman wears one in Lukiza(: 95), possibly with metaphoric meaning.
Praise not understood.
Kito, see note to 49.

I'd kill you and eat you...the-Spotted-One are two images of the lover's will-to-possess. The first (64-64.3) incorporates the loved object as food, as the seducer did in The Place You Come From(: 54-56). Here the desired consummation is inserted in a discourse of adornment and display - the beautiful eyes worn as beads, the bold refusal to honor the bond of cattle ownership that ultimately supports the state. In The Place You Come From the possession is enfolded in a discourse of familiarity. Familarity and adornment reappear in their respective plots as motivating themes. In the second image (65-65.2), the lover would possess the loved object as a prize, an achievement to be marked publically and poetically. (On the poetic resonances of the Spotted-one - here, the Leopard - see the note to Lukiza: 171.2) A groom's traditional recitation of self-praise, done on the day after his bride changes residence to consummate their marriage, is a social referent of the image; but uncultured men are also said to brag of their conquests, and this undertone is probably present as well.
Five line figure, 3 + 2, beginning and ending with a double verb pattern; the second and third lines begin with body parts, creating a 3-line abb figure in 64-64.2.
Stanza in aab formed by contrast between quoted utterances in the subjunctive (a's: 71, 71.1) and narration in the indicative (b: 71.2).
Stanza in aba' formed by repetition.
Stanza in aab formed by number sequence finalized by a non-numbered phrase.

The bard uses parts of this passage in other ballads (Kachwenyanja: 17.1-19.2 and Kajango: 52-52.1). Together, the passages show how he artfully recontextualizes the sequence. Here Muzee adds a metaphoric image to the four metonymic images of night that appear in Kachwenyanja. There, the passage was completed by an image of a plural subject acting at a particular place (courtiers dispersing from the palace) following a series of placeless single subjects (witch roaming, sick person fainting, lover meeting another). Here, the bard refinalizes the passage by adding an image with an abstract subject (love) that blossoms as two points (teeth) moving rhythmically together and apart (in a talking mouth). Note how the bard's phrasing can violate syntactic unity (79.1-80).
Stanza in aaaab formed semantically by a series of metonymic nighttime images (78-80.1) finalized by an extended metaphor (81-81.1).

Two stanzas in aabb; each begins with a double verb (83, 85); the four couplets that comprise the stanzas cohere variously, but of them the most marked form - repetition of initial locutives (86-86.1) - finalizes the passage as a whole.
The bargain-striker is an amount offered in barter that seals the deal, that brings the discussion of terms to a successful conclusion in preparation for the actual exchange of goods.PAGE

Verse in aab, 3 + 3 + 2, two 3-line stanzas finalized by a couplet; the two stanzas (87-88 and 89-91) are both structured as aab the first through contrast between two narrative lines and a single quoted utterance, the second through contrast between two initial verbs in the distant past and a single, initial, negative, past perfect; the couplet (92-93) coheres by repetition and enjambment.

A groom first receives his bride from a father's lap in a ceremony called kubukala (to hold on the lap, as one does to assist a woman in childbirth). This occurs as one of the many stages in a complete family wedding, which these characters circumvented by eloping. A bride would have been presented with waist beads just prior to the ceremony.
Stanza in abab formed semantically as bead names alternate with praise.
Straighten-your-legs are beads named for a pleasurable stretching movement associated with lovemaking.

Compound stanza in aaab, 2 + 2 + 2 + 2, formed by multiple couplets that cohere by virtue of semantic relationships (a's: general/specific (98-98.1), imperative/justification (99-100), crime/punishment (101-101.1)) contrasted with and a single, finalizing couplet that coheres by enjambment and the syntactic relationship between adverbial modification (102) and principal action (102.1); additionally, semantic contrasts between the three initial couplets and the final one - present/future and action/result - contribute to the aab pattern.

A drum is a complex symbol, described in the note to The Place You Come From:124. This statememt transforms a familiar way of swearing, from "...I'd lie with my own mother" to "...I'd lie with you." See Mugasha: 81.3-81.5, 86-86.2.
I'd lie with you is a statement that fulfills the form but not the content of punishment.
Great White is the Haya name for Victoria, and Bull refers to the god Mugasha.

To put the paddle on my hip is to propel the canoe. 115, 116: Kishwa, or Kishwagala, is the name of an epic bard from Ihangilo. His repertoire overlaps with Muzee's, and I have several performance texts of his in my collection.PAGE

Two stanzas (107-109.1 and 110-110.4); couplets in the first stanza cohere, respectively, by similarly inflected initial verbs (107-108) and by a repeated syntactic formula of initial nouns of apostrophe + negative imperatives (109-109.1); the structure of the first stanza concatenates the aabb pattern formed by coherent couplets with a pattern of abba formed by repetitions and replacements of "water"("lake") and "fire"; the second stanza is composed of two couplets (110-110.1 and 110.3-110.4) separated by a single line (110.2); the first couplet coheres by syntactic chaining of infinitives, while the second couplet coheres by virtue of initial verbs in the subjunctive; the 2 + 1 + 2 structure of the second stanza is ambiguously construed as aabb with an interpolated line or as a compound stanza in aba.
Stanza in abab formed by lexical contrast between yagya, "he went" and yagenda, "he travelled"

These two lines begin the elaborate principal Call of the ballad. They both contain strong narrative markers - adverbials and past perfect verbs. But with great drive, the bard rushes into them from the previous episode without a pause.

Stanza in concatenataed forms: aabb by virtue of couplets (139-140, 141-141.1) that cohere by parallelism and enjambment, respectively; and abba by contrast between lines in which a body part comes last (139, 141.1) and those in which it comes first (140, 141).
A shaven ring was the hairstyle of young women (see Kachwenyanja: 233-235). Its full name means "foretells illness," but I am not sure what this signifies.
Stanza in aba' formed by conversational turns and an initial locutive.

Stanza in aab(b); two parallel lines of quoted utterance (a's: 144-145) are finalized by a third that adds a verb ("I repeat")to the verb reiterated ("I desire"), thus creating a double verb pattern (b: 145.1); the figure is re-finalized with another marked contrast - a narrative line with a negative verb, embodying a shift in point of view.

The Haya word translated as gentle style is (o)bu-ntu, which has the same nominal root as omu-ntu "person" but the abstract prefix obu-. The word is cognate with utu in Kiswahili. "Personness" or "humanity" are other ways of translating the term.
The role of children in conveying a call evokes the ethical universe of a patrilineal clan, as it does in many versions of Lukiza.

Three couplets that cohere by enjambment (155-156); by initial locutives (157-158); and as parallel lines with repeated initial verbs (159-160); note that the finalizing couplet has the most marked coherence.
Calfs and goats... head for cropland because the preoccupied children are not concentrating on their usual domestic tasks.

Stanza in two couplets; the first (163-164) coheres by incremental repetition; it is joined to the second couplet (165-166) by syntactic linking (verb final to verb initial); the second couplet coheres internally by similarly inflected initial verbs; the quatrain is finalized by a double verb pattern.

Verse in aba; previous lines are initially repeated (167-168) and then augmented to form a linegroup (167-171) that is itself essentially repeated, forming a mirror stanza (174-177); the mirrored stanzas are separated by a couplet comprised of exclamations (172-173), forming the aba' pattern.

Child... you've brought a curse is an interchange that connotes danger (eihano). Children take on an adult role. They address a fully grown woman as "Child" (a familiar term of address derived from the word for "grandchild"), while she politely addresses them as adults, calling them "Mother." They believe the man they announce is a former lover "lost" by marriage but now returned - like the lover in The Place You Come From.

Compound stanza in aaab, 2 + 2 + 2 + 2; couplets cohere by enjambment (177.1-178) and by parallelism (178.1-178.2, 179-179.1); the final couplet differs by its semantic non-coherence: the wife's response (181) markedly ignores the children's message.
Words in epic verse - literally, "words or matters in the enanga (tradition) - are paradoxically without real substance. They do not come from the heart of a speaker, as these words of love do.

The first eleven lines of seductive compliments substantially repeat those spoken by the woman's husband when he was courting her to elope (62.1.-63.1). The utterances of the husband and of the lover differ in the lines that precede or follow each passage. The husband's speech expresses his will to possess the object of his desire completely (as by eating: 64), exclusively (even against the laws of the state: 64.3) and publically (through self-adornment: 64.1 and self-praise: 65-65.2). It articulates a clan-related ethics of alliance - of a woman thoroughly wedded to the self of a man publicly and privately. The lover's speech, on the contrary, appreciates the social and aesthetic virtues of the young woman - the way she looks (139-141.1), the way she moves (160), and the demeanor she projects (145). The lover's seductive speech also articulates a discourse of rapture (194-200.1), represented as a state like that produced by alcohol, marijuana, or madness. His persona is both lost in her beauty and conscious of its significance. He knows the themes of passionate love.

Stanza in aab(b); the parallelism of the first two lines (189, 190) is finalized in the third (190.1) by a double verb formation and then re-finalized by another double verb with a shift in inflection (see 144-146).
Two stanzas with mirror structure (194-197, 198-200.1): accusation (194, 198) elaboration of charge (195-194.1, 199), denial (196, 200), plea (197, 200.1).

Verse passage in aab composed of two stanzas (202-205 and 206-209) finalized by a single line (210); the first stanza is formed as aabb by parallelism, by semantic contrasts between kin and non-kin (children and brides versus warriors and the semi-rich) and by the presence of the term of address maawe, "my mother"; the second stanza is structured as abab by virtue of a repeated formula, banana name/ "sister of [color modifier] people"; the single line (210) finalizes the passage by expressing the desire that motivates the praise.
Compound stanza in aabb, 2 + 2 + 2 + 2, by repetition.

The abstinence taboo circumscribes a woman's behavior with men other than her husband. As in the present case, if a man goes on a journey and his wife takes a lover, the husband becomes vulnerable. He may fall ill or die, or his enterprise may be unsuccessful. Or he may die from having intercourse with his wife on his return if she has not used the proper medicines. This prohibition also includes other kinds of behavior, like a wife's giving ensenene - highly valued, seasonally available, fried or parched grasshoppers - to another man when her husband is away; the ensenene she gives to her husband on his return will kill him. And it affects other family members: if a child has not cut teeth and a woman sleeps with a lover, the teeth will not grow properly or the child may get sick and die. Such supernatural sanctions to not encumber men.
Stanza in abab by repetition.

Compound stanza in aab, 2 + 3 + 1; initial linegroups (a's: 221-222, 223-224.1) are formed by combinations of single verb initial lines (221, 223, 224) finalized by double verb initial lines (222, 224.1); the transition between these linegroups is marked by chaining (222-223); the figure is finalized by a line that has no verb (b: 225).
Tongue-cutter, Call-and-they-come, and Bolt-for-the-door are names for herbs.
Water brought for washing signifies domesticity, family solidarity, and wife-like obedience (see, for example, Lukiza: 47, 101.3; Kachwenyanja: 394).
Stanza in abcb formed by parallel use of relative clauses and repetition of /nab/ "wash."
Stanza in aab formed by contrast between initial, 2nd person subjunctive verbs (a's:230, 231) and final, third person negative (b: 232).[ka discount]

By raising a column in his father's house, a son defined a sleeping space for his new bride and himself. Later on, if he is not the youngest son (who inherits the paraent's house), he would build a house of his own.PAGE
Stanza in abab, formed by repetition of the formula, verb + direct object/ negative verb.
Stanza in abab formed by repetition of the formula, negative subjunctive verb + direct object/ future verb + prepositional phrase; note that word order is reversed in 238.
In a bad way, a fortnight of anticipation, garden mounds in disorder, and the steps are all euphemisms for menstruation.

Compound stanza in aab, 2 + 2 + 2, formed by contrast between couplets that cohere by parallel syntax (a's: 239-240 (verbal inflection) and 241-241.1 (prepositional phrase)) and one that coheres by lexical repetition (b: 242-242.1, "I have 'the steps'"); the latter also contrasts to the former couplets semantically in being considered a more direct naming of menstruation.
Stanza in aab by contrast between similarly inflected initial narrative verbs (243, 243.1) and a quoted utterance that begins with a pronoun.

Compound stanza in aba, 2 + 2 + 2, by contrast between couplets composed according to the formula, ...2nd person subjunctive + preposition/...first person subjunctive + direct object (a's: 245-245.2, 247-247.1) and a single couplet (b: 246-246.1) with a similar, but variant syntax.
Smile refers to a recently shaved pubes; see note to 15.
Stanza in aab formed by repeated verbal inflection.

Stanza in aabb formed by lines in which nouns are in foregrounded positions (first (256), last (257), and as a direct object preceding a verb (258)) contrasted with a line that is solely composed of verbs (258.1).

Get out of here! The response of the sister-in-law (with whom a bride traditionally enjoys an intimate relationship) is to berate the young woman for even thinking she might not have an affair with so worthy a lover, a neighbor whom the sister-in-law evidently knows. Wives, are you not begged? she asks rhetorically, using a euphemism for sexual intercourse. Wind represents the insubstantial, worthless lovers wives have liaisons with when they go out to the fields between villages to cultivate. They are as wind, compared with the knowing neighbor.

Compound stanza in aab, 2 + 2 + 2, formed by contrast between two couplets that cohere by virtue of a linear relationship (259-260, 261-262) and a single couplet (263-264) that coheres solely by strict parallelism and temporal simultaneity.

Two verses in aab (259-271, 272-287) comprised of six compound stanzas described immediately above, formed by contrast between compound stanzas that are substantially repeated (a's: 259-264, 265-267.1 repeated in 272-277, 278-280.1, respectively) and compound stanzas that are not repeated (b's: 268-271, 282-287).

The sister-in-law's charm to protect against the abstinence taboo begins with images of land and crops to be protected: earth, stone, and winnowing-basket. Then she advises about care of the bride's body (268-271). A storage-pole (fixed near the cooking fire and used to store and display vessels) and milk pots are symbols associated with the bride's sexuality. Both body and land are central to reproduction of a clan.

Compound stanza in aab, 2 + 2 + 2, formed by contrast between two couplets that cohere by virtue of shared syntactic features (prepositional phrases - 265-265.1, double verb formation - 265.2-266) and a single couplet that coheres by strict parallelism (267-267.1).

Compound stanza in aab, 2 + 2 + 2, formed by two couplets that cohere by virtue of shared syntactic features (direct object, hairstyle, in final position - 268-268.1, and similarly inflected verbs in initial position - 269-269.1) contrasted with a single couplet that coheres more tightly by shared double verb formation and closely related direct objects in final position - milk vessels and the pole they hang from; the last couplet is also distinctive in being a possible metaphor for sexual matters.

Compound stanza in aab, 2 + 2 + 2, a substantial repetition of lines 259-264; contrast between finalizing couplet and the initial couplets remains essentially the same and a double verb has been added so that the pattern now marks both initial couplets (272, 274).

Compound stanza in aab, 2 + 2 + 2, a substantial repetition of lines 265-267.1; contrast between the finalizing couplet and the initial two couplets remains substantially the same; the contrast has been strengthened by removing the verb from the first line (278 compared to 265) making the initial couplet now solely prepositional phrases.

The figurative advice of the bride's nephew (her husband's brother's son) draws a distinction between pain and killing. The former is associated with love and desire (the soul serpent: 284-285), the latter with reproduction and order (body: 283 and spear: 286-287). Making a bed to have an affair (288) does not kill: individual passion does not have lasting significance (only clan duty does). PAGE

Compound stanza in aab, 2 + 2 + 2, formed by contrast between two couplets that cohere by virtue of parallelism (lexical - "...kills not./...what kills is..." -283-284, and semantic - "What pricks.../It pains..." - 284-285) and a single finalizing couplet (186-187) that coheres by an enjambment marked by a direct object preceeding its verb and by a double verb formation.
Stanza in aabb in which couplets cohere internally by enjambment and repetition, respectively, and relate to each other as utterance and response.
Stanza in aabb in which couplets cohere internally by repetition (/zala/ "bear" or "mother" - 295-295.1 and /lemba/ "beguile" 296-296.1) and relate to each other as conversational turns.

Compound stanza in aab, 2 + 2 + 1, formed by couplets that cohere internally by repetition (a's: "elder" - 297-298; "child" - 299-299.1) contrasted with a single line that repeats elements from both couplets (b: 300).

The doorway arch of bundled bamboo is a beautiful architectural feature of an old-style Haya house (msonge). The backs of many older women in Hayaland have become permanently bent, people say, from the heavy agricultural digging they do with a short handled hoe.

Compound stanza in abab, 2 + 1 + 2 + 3, formed by contrast between linegroups that specify conditions (a's: 301-302, 304-305) and those that state result (b's: 303, 106-106.2); syntactic coherence is strongest in the first couplet, which combines infinitive and distant past in each line, and in the contrast between halves - the first ab being couched in the distant past, the second in the past perfect, except for the final line (306.2) that combines the tenses.
The address, Child, reverses the ages of the speaker and addressee in a gesture of familiarity (see 169).
They did not lie to you when they said you'd grow up well.
Stanza in aabb, whose couplets cohere by enjambment and syntactic chaining of direct objects, respectively. [discount]
Coffee berries, cooked with spices and preserved, are eaten as a snack and mild stimulant.
Ngalama is the name of a village. It is also a pun on oku-galama "to lie down."
Stanza in aabbb whose couplet and triplet cohere by chaining and whose transition between linegroups is marked by a double verb formation.
The intense interchange in bickering indicates the character's goal. The word can also have a negative connotation, as in Kajango: 4.

A triplet (319.1-320) and a couplet (321-322) are repeated (325-325.2 and 324-324.1, respectively) to form an annular pattern (alternately, a compounded aba structure); the triplet coheres by semantic relationships between imperative (319) and justification (319.2-320); the couplet coheres by syntactic formula (cattle descriptor + 2nd person verb - 321-322); and the couplet that stands at the center of the figure (323-323.1) coheres by enjambment.[discount]
The image of fighting bulls pictures an active, robust female sexuality. The older woman remembers her past.
Stanza in aabb whose couplets cohere internally by repeated double verb formation and lexical repetition, respectively.

Five of the first six lines name herbs that also appear in the bard's Kajango: 175-175.6. The remaining six lines name herbs and describe effects to create a poetic figure on the experience of rapture.
Stanza in aabb whose couplets cohere internally by repeated initial verbal forms (adverbial prefix - 337-338; distant past - 339-339.1)
Off-they-race is a small earthenware vessel used in feminine preparations for lovemaking. Its name celebrates its ability to heat up quickly (see Kajango: 180-180.1).
Stanza in aaab formed by contrast between lines that begin with verbs similarly inflected and a finalizing line of praise that begins with an aside.
The wonders that begin here have been ritually and socially prepared so as to avoid unlucky consequences. They are still called amahano, a term that connotes their danger (see note to 45-47).

Stanza in aab(b) formed by lines that begin with double verbs in 1st person singular (342, 342.1) contrasted with a line that is wholly comprised of a 1st person plural verb (343); the figure is then refinalized with a contrasting line that wholly lacks a verb (344) and that supplies a detail confirming the psychological intimacy of the embrace - a right handed (rather than a left handed) caress.

Compound stanza in aab(b), 2 + 2 + 1 (+ 1), formed by couplets composed according to a formula, 1st person singular narration/praise or apposition (a's: 345-346, order reversed in 347-348) finalized by a single line in 3rd person singular narration (b: 349); the figure is then refinalized by a single line in the 3rd person plural that speaks of more physically intimate contact below.

Compound stanza in abab, 1 + 2 + 1 + 2, formed by alternation of single lines (a's: 351, 354) that introduce couplets (b's: 352-353, 355-356) by chaining the word /jum/ "verbal(ly) abuse"; couplets cohere, respectively, by repetition and parallelism and by enjambment (cause/effect or crime/punishment).

The bride uses the same rhetorical form that her husband used earlier with slight, but significant, variation. Maawe, "Mother" (354), a term of address, seems to be a mistake, for these lines are spoken by the bride to her lover.
Stanza in aabb formed by couplets that cohere by repetition and by enjambment, respectively; the figure coheres as a whole by enjambment.

Verse in aab composed of 3 stanzas in aab of three lines each (367.1-369, 370-372, 373-374.1): the contrasts between a's and b's are semantic throughout (man/house as subject, change in speaker, and act of naming/justification); initial couplets cohere by enjambment in the first two stanzas but by marked parallelism in the third, a contrast the creates the aab form of the verse as a whole.
Stanza in abab formed by repetition and parallelism.
snake - see note to Lukiza: 171.2.
Stanza in aaaaaab formed by repetition and parallelism; the final line (385) is shortened to create the contrast.
Sweet mash is similar to the metaphor another bard uses (see The Place You Come From: 72).

Dramatic punishments for unnatural deeds (amahano) seem to be part of a discourse of deterrence. See, for example, the ending to the story, "Child of the Valley" in See so... and the remembered punishment for violating clan exogamy in Cory and Hartnoll: 92.
Ashes for friction and a slap to disguise the pain are aspects of one method used to depilate a vulva (see note to 12-15).

Compound stanza in aab, 3 + 2 + 4, formed by linegroups that cohere syntactically (subjunctive + linking - 400-402; subjunctive verbs initial - 403-404) contrasted with a line group that is distinctive semantically as praise.

Because of their ease of movement, birds witness and mediate secret acts in narratives. This element seems purposely to leave the tale slightly incomplete: what if the news should reach the husband when he returns?
This last aside seems to convey the same kind of pessimism the bard leaves us with in Kajango.
